questionText: A curve is defined by $x = 3\tan t$ and $y = 2\sec t$, where $t$ is a parameter. (a) Use an appropriate identity to find a Cartesian equation of the curve. (b) Identify the curve and state the coordinates of its vertices and the equations of its asymptotes. (c) Explain why no point of the curve satisfies $-2 < y < 2$.

steps.explanation: We want an identity linking $\tan t$ and $\sec t$, so first make each the subject. Here $\tan t = x/3$.
Likewise $\sec t = y/2$ from the second equation.
This Pythagorean-type identity connects exactly the two functions we have, so it will eliminate $t$.
Replacing $\tan t$ and $\sec t$ by their $x$ and $y$ expressions removes the parameter.
Squaring the fractions tidies the equation and prepares it for standard form.
Moving the $x$-term across leaves the difference of two squared terms equal to $1$ — the signature of a hyperbola.
This matches $\dfrac{y^{2}}{b^{2}} - \dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}} = 1$ with $b = 2$ and $a = 3$: a hyperbola centred at the origin that opens up and down, because the $y^{2}$ term is the positive one.
The vertices lie on the axis of symmetry, found by setting $x=0$. They are the points of the two branches closest to the centre.
These are the tips of the upper and lower branches.
For large $x$ and $y$ the constant $1$ becomes negligible, so the curve hugs the lines obtained by replacing $1$ with $0$.
The two branches approach these straight lines but never touch them.

steps.description: Analyse the values of sec t
steps.explanation: Since $\sec t = 1/\cos t$ and $|\cos t| \le 1$, the secant can never lie strictly between $-1$ and $1$.
Multiplying the secant bounds by $2$ shows $y$ is always at least $2$ or at most $-2$.
So the strip $-2 < y < 2$ contains no part of the curve — consistent with the two separate branches of a hyperbola whose vertices sit at $y = \pm 2$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $\dfrac{y^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{x^{2}}{9} = 1$. (b) A hyperbola centred at the origin opening vertically, with vertices $(0,\,2)$ and $(0,\,-2)$ and asymptotes $y = \pm\dfrac{2}{3}x$. (c) Because $y = 2\sec t$ and $|\sec t| \ge 1$, we always have $y \ge 2$ or $y \le -2$, so no point lies in $-2 < y < 2$.
